dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to a male patient with chest pain at --Address Redacted-- in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. What's been going on today?
I'm having left-sided chest pain. It's sharp, under, and around my left breast. Rated an 8 out of 10.
You had some diaphoresis at first. Has the sweating stopped, and is the pain still there?
Yes, the sweat stopped, but the chest pain has remained.
Tell me your name, the date, the city, and who I am.
Name's Fred. Date is December 22. City is [CITY], and you're an EMT.
You are alert and oriented x4. Good. Let me check your pupillary response.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive-pearl bilaterally.
Skin is normal, warm, and dry. No bruising, discoloration, droop, slurring, or facial asymmetry noted.
Mr. Fred, I'm taking your blood pressure now.
Blood pressure is 152/78. Pulse is 68, respiratory rate 16.
Oxygen saturation is 100% on room air.
Let me check your blood sugar now.
Glucose is at 115. Appears stable.
I'm connecting you to the EKG monitor now.
The 4-lead EKG shows a paced rhythm with occasional PACs and PVCs-monitor is interpreting as sinus rhythm due to the pacer.
Completed a 12-lead EKG for hospital interpretation as well.
The 12-lead EKG also shows the same result with a nonspecific T wave abnormality.
You are 63 years old, male, and weigh 91 kg. Can confirm?
For documentation, could you share your full address?
It's --Address Redacted--, Apartment Number, outside of [CITY].
I've given you 324 milligrams of aspirin. It helps prevent blood clots in potential cardiac events.
Normal saline IV has been started in your left hand via a 20-gauge needle.
You have a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a pacemaker. Correct?
Yes, that's accurate.
You reported no head or neck injuries. Facial features are symmetrical with no droop noted.
No reported visual problems. No observed neck stiffness.
No lymphadenopathy or neck masses detected. Pupils remain equal and reactive.
No drainage, obstruction, or foreign bodies in the airway. Hearing seems fine.
No signs of respiratory distress. No jugular venous distension (JVD) noted.
Lung sounds are clear. No wheezing, crackles, or decreased breath sounds.
No abdominal pain or distension reported. Bowel sounds not assessed.
No deformities, angulation, or wounds to the extremities.
Pt has good pulses to all extremities (PMS) and sensation. Motor and sensory functions remain intact.
Back and flank appear normal. Vertebral column unremarkable.
Per your request and availability, we will transport you to [COMPANY_NAME].
You tolerated the trip well. Pain improved slightly en route.
Secured you safely with all available safety straps during transport.
You were alert and oriented x4 throughout the ride.
Vital signs included 152/78, 68, 16; second reading was 180/80, 74, 16, spo2 at 98% by oximetry.
Oxygen saturation dropped slightly to 98% during transport but remains stable.
Administration of aspirin was per protocol. IV remains running at this point.
Provided supportive care en route to manage your pain and maintain your vitals.
Mr. Fred presented alert and oriented x4 with no GI distress or fever.
No respiratory distress or JVD. Lung sounds clear. No reported GI issues.
All extremities showed good motor and sensory function, no trauma or swelling reported.
Patient was secured on the stretcher and transferred using his own strength into the ER bed.
He signed himself as the responsible party. We handed over patient care and report to the nurse.
Thank you for the full report and detailed care provided.
Airway is patent and maintainable. No signs of obstruction or sounds of stridor.
No signs of cyanosis, tracheal deviation, or work of breathing noted.
You have equal and symmetrical chest movement with no flaring of your nostrils.
Peripheral pulses are palpable with normal skin temperature and appearance.
Blood pressure elevated to 180/80 on second assessment. Recommend continued monitoring.
No signs of rashes, purpura, petechiae, or abnormal skin turgor noted.
No reported swallowing difficulties or memory deficits.
No nuchal rigidity or neck masses detected.
No signs of sunken eyes or signs of hearing loss to assess.
Heart sounds were normal, no murmurs or extrasystoles noted.
Comparison of peripheral and central pulses came back normal.
No palpable masses or suprapubic discomfort reported.
No reported rectal bleeding or changes in urine color or frequency.
No signs of urgency or lower abdominal discomfort.
Movement is smooth and without resistance or complaints of pain.
Vertebral column, flank, and buttocks show no signs of injury.
Respiratory rate remains at 16 breaths per minute, stable and regular.
The EKG monitor will continue to run for the trip to the hospital.
Spo2 remains at about 98%. No signs of hypoxia.
Glucose readings remained within normal range at 115 mg/dL.
You said the pain rated 8/10 started around 23:00.
Nothing makes it worse or better, you said. That's important to note.
You described the pain as sharp. Any other descriptions?
The pain is localized under and around your left breast. No radiation elsewhere.
You said 8/10. Let's reassess after a few minutes.
Pain has been consistent in timing and nature. No changes noted yet.
Tell us again when the pain started and what you were doing at the time.
I was sitting on the bench, eating pizza and watching TV. Then chest pain started.
Describe the diaphoresis you mentioned earlier.
I had some sweating around the same time the pain started. It stopped, but pain remained.
No known allergies to report.
You're on lisinopril, sotalol, atorvastatin, prednisone, and codeine. Understand that.
You have a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia-important for your care.
You also have a cardiac pacemaker. Let's make sure rapid hospital transport is arranged.
Last meal was about an hour ago-pepperoni pizza.
No nausea, vomiting, or changes in appetite reported.
You had no trauma or event-baseline was stable before the chest pain.
Aspirin given at 23:30, IV started at the same time with NS.
Transporting under protocol 2-1, medical - chest pain - cardiac suspected.
Reason for selection: EKG findings, age (>40), and male sex with chest pain.
Reassessing pain ratings and blood pressure every 5 minutes en route.
EKG readings will be repeated every 15 minutes to monitor for cardiac changes.
Oxygen saturation and pulse will be retaken regularly.
Vital signs showed SBP increase to 180mmHg and O2 sat minor decrease.
No significant cardiac arrhythmias or signs of MI noted in the meantime.
Rechecked medications-ASA and IV administered properly with no noted adverse effects.
Mr. Fred remained coherent and calm during the drive.
No new changes in patient status-stable under transport conditions.
Repeating summary: same status upon arrival and last assessment with no new interventions.
Patient secured for hospital transfer using same methods as for roadside limb.
